coredata.dc:title: An ultrasensitive lateral flow immunoassay platform for foodborne biotoxins and pathogenic bacteria based on carbon-dots embedded mesoporous silicon nanoparticles fluorescent reporter probes
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yuhui
Deng, Chen
Qian, Sihua
Li, Hui
Fu, Pan
Zhou, Hualan
Zheng, Jianping
coredata.dc:description: Lateral flow immunoassays (LFIAs) are routine methods for rapid foodborne pollutants screening, with detection limits that are closely associated with the label probes used. The exploitation of high performance and robust probe is highly desirable, and remains a great challenge. Herein, we reported an emerging fluorescent nanobeads i.e. carbon-dots (CD) covalently incorporated mesoporous silicon nanoparticles (CD-MSNs) for LFIAs. CD-MSNs revealed brighter fluorescence, larger particle size and more modification sites in comparison with those of single CD. After bio-functionalisation, CD-MSNs probes were introduced to construct LFIA test strips, and designed for ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), two representative foodborne pollutants, based on the competitive and sandwich models, respectively. Very competitive quantitative detection limits i.e. 0.05 ng/mL and 102 cfu/mL were correspondingly obtained. Additionally, the test strips were successfully applied to rapidly and accurately screen AFB1 and S. aureus in food samples, highlighting their practicality.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-Dots
Mesoporous silicon nanoparticles
Fluorescence probe
Lateral flow immunoassay
Foodborne pollutants

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparations of silanized CDs, DMSNs and CD-MSNs.
Surface modification and bioconjugation of CD-MSNs
AFB1 and S. aureus sensing via the test strips
Fabrication and detection principles of the CD-MSN-based FLFAs
Characterizations of silanized CDs, DMSNs, and CD-MSNs
Demonstrating CD-MSN surface modification and bioconjugation
Analytical performance of CD-MSN LFA test strips
Rapid AFB1 and S. aureus screening in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ningbo Science Technology Innovation 2025 Major Research Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support from Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation ( LY20B050003 ), Ningbo 3315 Innovation Teams Program (2019A-14-C), and Ningbo Science Technology Innovation 2025 Major Research Project (2018B10054).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image and (b) the corresponding Gaussian-fitted particle-size distribution of the harvested CDs, along with their (c) UV–vis absorption spectrum and (d) fluorescence spectra acquired at various excitation wavelengths.

TEM images of (a) DMSNs and (b) CD-MSNs. (c) Enlarged image of the selected area in panel (b). EDS spectra of (d) DMSNs and (e) CD-MSNs. (f) FTIR spectra of silanized CDs, DMSNs and CD-MSNs. (g) Zeta potential of the CD-MSNs nanospheres. (h) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the CD-MSNs. (i) Fluorescence emission spectra of the CD-MSNs in aqueous solution (20 µg/mL) at different excitation wavelengths.

(a) FTIR spectrum of the CD-MSNs nanospheres. (b) Zeta potentials of MSNs, CD-MSNs, COOH-CD-MSNs, CD-MSNs-mAb, and CD-MSNs-Van, respectively. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of CD-MSNs and COOH-CD-MSNs at the same concentration (10 µg/mL) under identical experimental conditions.

(a) Kinetics curves for the CD-MSN AFB1 test strip (PB and 20 ng/mL AFB1 spiked PB were taken as negative and positive samples, respectively). (b) Linear relationship between the ST/SC ratio and the AFB1 concentration in the 0.05–10 ng/mL range. (c) Test strip selectivity toward AFB1 (AFB1 and the inspected substances: 10 ng/mL). (d) Kinetics curve for the CD-MSN S. aureus test strip (PB and 105 cfu/mL S. aureus spiked PB were treated as negative and positive samples, respectively). (e) Linear relationship between ST/SC and the logarithm of the S. aureus concentrations in the 102 to 106 cfu/mL range. (f) Selectivity of the test strip towards S. aureus (bacteria: 105 cfu/mL). All error bars represent standard deviations of three parallel experiments.

Schematic showing the preparations of (a) silanized CDs and (b) the CD-MSNs fluorescent label probes (i.e., CD-MSNs-mAb and CD-MSNs-Van). (c) Competitive and (d) sandwich principles for the detection of AFB1 and S. aureus, respectively, by the CD-MSN LFA test strips.
Detecting AFB1 in real sample using the CD-MSN LFA test strip (n = 3).

head.ce:title: An ultrasensitive lateral flow immunoassay platform for foodborne biotoxins and pathogenic bacteria based on carbon-dots embedded mesoporous silicon nanoparticles fluorescent reporter probes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Cixi Institute of Biomedical Engineering, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Ningbo 315300, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Cixi Institute of Biomedical Engineering, Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology & Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS), Ningbo 315300, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Health Science and Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Health Science and Engineering
University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Health Science and Engineering, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Oil Crops Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Wuhan 430062, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Oil Crops Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Wuhan 430062, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lateral flow immunoassays (LFIAs) are routine methods for rapid foodborne pollutants screening, with detection limits that are closely associated with the label probes used. The exploitation of high performance and robust probe is highly desirable, and remains a great challenge. Herein, we reported an emerging fluorescent nanobeads i.e. carbon-dots (CD) covalently incorporated mesoporous silicon nanoparticles (CD-MSNs) for LFIAs. CD-MSNs revealed brighter fluorescence, larger particle size and more modification sites in comparison with those of single CD. After bio-functionalisation, CD-MSNs probes were introduced to construct LFIA test strips, and designed for ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), two representative foodborne pollutants, based on the competitive and sandwich models, respectively. Very competitive quantitative detection limits i.e. 0.05 ng/mL and 102 cfu/mL were correspondingly obtained. Additionally, the test strips were successfully applied to rapidly and accurately screen AFB1 and S. aureus in food samples, highlighting their practicality.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lateral flow assays (LFAs) are ideal detection platforms for point-of-care testing (POCT) owing to the outstanding merits of affordability, user-friendliness, sensitivity, specificity, and screening rapidity (Liu, Zhan, Qiu, Sackrison, & Bischof, 2021). LFAs have attracted broad interest in many fields, including clinical examination and food safety (Chen, Zhang, Jin et al., 2020; Chen, Zhang, Zhai et al., 2020; Tang et al., 2017). Their detection targets are numerous and can be classified as small molecules (Pratt, Fan, Melakeberhan, & Klapperich, 2018), biomacromolecules (Wang et al., 2020), microorganisms (Shan, Lai, Xiong, Wei, & Xu, 2015), and viruses (Chen, Zhang, Jin et al., 2020; Chen, Zhang, Zhai et al., 2020). LFAs are designed to operate using competitive and sandwich models as two classic design principles that typically rely on immune recognition and nucleic-acid binding (Bahadr & Sezgintürk, 2016). Colloidal gold nanoparticles (NPs) are routinely used as label probes in LFAs; their excellent stabilities, facile syntheses, and result-visualisation capabilities have led to their commercialisation (Serebrennikova, Samsonova, & Osipov, 2018). Despite these advantages, naked-eye-based colorimetric analysis and its relatively low detection sensitivity and the possibility of generating false-negative results are inherent drawbacks (Deng, Jiang, Li, & Lv, 2021; Shah & Yager, 2017).

Probe labelling can significantly influence the LFA detection limit (Quesada-Gonzalez & Merkoci, 2015). To date, diverse nanomaterials, including magnetic Fe3O4 NPs (Wang et al., 2019), carbon materials (Wiriyachaiporn, Sirikett, Maneeprakorn, & Dharakul, 2017), polymer NPs (Liu, Li, & Yang, 2020), fluorescent dyes (Wang et al., 2021), semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (Huang et al., 2018), and up-conversion phosphors (UCPs) (Liang et al., 2017), have been developed as signal reporters for LFA applications with the aim of enhancing sensitivity. Indeed, integrating them into nanospheres (e.g., SiO2 and polystyrene) greatly improves sensitivity compared to single QD or fluorescent dyes, owing to remarkably enhanced fluorescence intensities (Huang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021). While significant progress toward the construction and application of fluorescent nanospheres has been achieved, some limitations have been reported, including complicated preparation procedures, leakage of the phosphor from nanosphere pores when suspended in nonpolar media, and possible aggregation during polymerisation (Wilson, Spiller, Prior, Bhatt, & Hutchinson, 2007). Therefore, exploiting new fluorescent nanospheres for LFIA applications is a meaningful but challenging task.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) are emerging inorganic phosphors with zero-dimensional carbon structures (Liu, Dou et al., 2020). Compared to QDs and organic fluorescent dyes, CDs are more chemically inert, show unique photoelectric characteristics, are essentially non-cytotoxic, very water soluble, can be facilely synthesised, and their surfaces can be readily modified (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015). As a result, CDs are generally used in chemo/biosensing (Ji, Zhou, Leblanc, & Peng, 2020), bioimaging (Xu et al., 2018), nanotheranostics applications (Jia et al., 2020), among others (Peng et al., 2022), and have been shown to be promising and competitive label probe candidates. However, single CD fluoresce relatively poorly, have limited modifiable sites, and are not suitable for LFA applications (Xu, Zhang, Ding, Xu, & Chen, 2019).

Foodborne toxins and pathogenic microorganisms pose great threats to human health (Cui, Ye, Ping, & Sun, 2020). Despite the detection limits of current LFA-based test strips for foodborne toxins and pathogenic microorganisms are relatively high (Tang et al., 2017), the development of low-cost, sensitive, rapid, and on-site detection methods is essential for monitoring food safety (Zhang et al., 2019). Inspired by the above, herein, we report the development of a new label probe, namely CD-embedded dendritic mesoporous silicon-nanoparticle (CD-MSNs) fluorescent nanobeads, and their use in LFAs for the rapid screening of foodborne pollutants. Owing to their unique structures that endow them with high specific surface areas, large pore volumes, adjustable pore sizes, and V-shaped pore structures, MSNs are ideal nanocarriers for loading phosphors when forming fluorescent nanospheres (Huang et al., 2018; Tang, Li, & Chen, 2012). This covalent incorporation of high-density CDs into MSNs enables no leakage, and thus stable as well as very intense fluorescence emissions are obtained. Meanwhile, the larger particles provide abundant sites for functionalization.

Taking AFB1 and S. aureus as representative targets, we constructed two LFA test strips, one built on the competitive model and the other on the sandwich model, using CD-MSNs nanobeads as the reporter probes. Owing to the outstanding fluorescence performance of the CD-MSNs (e.g., high quantum yield, strong and stable emission), the fabricated test strips displayed extremely low detection limits and great potential for the rapid screening of AFB1 and S. aureus in food samples. Moreover, this work will provide opportunities for exploiting a sensing platform for other species by ligand-modifying the CD-MSNs.

Rose bengal (RB) sodium salt (≥85 %), goat anti-mouse IgG and vancomycin (Van) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. anti-AFB1monoclonal antibody (mAbs) were purchased from Santa Cruz Biotechnology, Inc. anti-S. aureus antibody was purchased from Abcam. Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), N-[3-(Trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (TMS-EDA), sodium salicylate, tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), triethanolamine (TEA), aflatoxin B1 (AFB2), zearalenone (ZEN), ochratoxin A (OTA) sulfadimethoxine (SDM), 2-(N-morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES) were from Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). AFB1 was from J&K Chemical Co., ltd (Beijing, China). Acetamiprid (AAP), kanamycin (KAN), 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), succinic anhydride, bovine serum albumin (BSA), ammonia and ethyl acetate were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Nitrocellulose (NC) membranes (Sartorius CN140) were supplied by Sartorius Stedim Biotech GmbH (Germany). The sample pads (RB65), absorbent pad (glass fiber 8975) and PVC plate (DB-6) were from Jieyi Biotechnology Co., ltd (Shanghai, China). S. aureus, E. coli DH5α, E. coli O157:H7, Bacillus thuringiensis, S. epidermidis and Proteus mirabilis were from our laboratory.

The sizes and morphologies of CDs, MSNs and CD-MSNs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai F20). Fluorescence spectra were measured by a Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer (LS-55). The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a spectrophotometer of UV-2600i (Shimadzu). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on Nicolet (Magan-IR Spectrometer 500) using the KBr pellet technique. The absolute fluorescence quantum yields were determined by a Fluoromax-4 measurement system (HORIBA). Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern) was applied for the measurements of size distribution and surface potential. A HGS510 dispensing platform and fluorescence quantitative analyzer (FIC-Q1) were purchased from Hangzhou Fenghang Technology Co., ltd. (Hangzhou, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations of silanized CDs, DMSNs and CD-MSNs.
ce:section.ce:para: Silanized CDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method (Chen et al., 2018). In brief, 30 mg RB was dissolved in 9 mL H2O, 1 mL TMS-EDA was added subsequently. After a 30 min magnetic stirring, the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon autoclave and heated to 160 °C for 4 h at an oven. After being cooled to room temperature, the crude product was filtered with a 0.2 μm filter membrane and then dialyzed (MW. 500 Da). The solution was dried by freeze, and a solid power i.e. silanized CDs was obtained.

DMSNs were prepared by a classical surfactant templating method (Yang et al., 2016). Briefly, 68 mg TEA was dispersed in 25 mL H2O, and stirred at 80 °C for 30 min. Then, 380 mg CTAB and 168 mg sodium salicylate were added into the above solution, and completely mixed via a vigorous magnetic stirring for 1 h. After that, 4 mL TEOS was injected and kept stirring at 80 °C for 2 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction solution was diluted with ethanol and the precipitate was collected by centrifugation. The precipitate was washed several times with ethanol, and finally dispersed in a HCl/ethanol solution at 60 °C and refluxed for 24 h to extract the residue of the organic template. Finally, after being centrifuged and washed by ethanol and water for three times, DMSNs were harvested and kept in ethanol.

CD-MSNs were prepared by the covalent link between silanized CDs and DMSNs. In brief, 5 mg of silanized CDs was added into 50 mL of ethanol containing 50 mg of DMSNs, and sonicated for 10 min. Then, 1.25 mL of ammonia was added and stirred for a 12 h reaction. Finally, the resulting precipitate i.e. the CD-MSNs product was collected via centrifugation, and purified by washing with water and ethanol for several times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification and bioconjugation of CD-MSNs
ce:section.ce:para: Surface modification of CD-MSNs was routinely achieved according to the previous report (Amaral, Lopes, Santos, & Silva, 2002). Briefly, CD-MSNs (20 mg) was dissolved in 20 mL ethanol, 0.2 mL APTES was added subsequently. After an overnight reaction at room temperature, the amino-CD-MSNs was harvested through centrifugation and washing by water and ethanol.

For subsequent surface carboxyl functionalization, amino-CD-MSNs (20 mg) and succinic anhydride (25 mg) were dispersed in 5 mL anhydrous DMF, and stirred for a 2 h reaction. Then, the solution was centrifuged and washed twice with water. The final precipitate i.e. carboxyl-modified CD-MSNs (COOH-CD-MSNs) was collected and dissolved in water.

The bioconjugation of CD-MSNs to antibody was performed following our reported method (Tang et al., 2019). In brief, COOH-CD-MSNs (0.5 mg) dispersed in MES buffer (10 mM, pH. 6.1) was mixed with 60 μL EDC (10 mg/mL) and 30 μL NHS (10 mg/mL) to activate the carboxyl for 30 min. Then, pH value of the above reaction solution was adjusted to 7.5, and with 20 μg of anti-AFB1 antibody added. After being incubated for 4 h, the product i.e. CD-MSNs-mAb was obtained by centrifugation with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH.7.4) for two times, and finally dispersed in 1 mL PBS and stored at 4 °C icebox for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFB1 and S. aureus sensing via the test strips
ce:section.ce:para: 50 μL standard solution with different concentrations of targets and 20 μL CD-MSNs label probe were added into a 96-well plate for 5 min. The strips were then inserted into the above mixtures and reacted for 30 min. The paper strip was loaded with plastic clamps, and the visualization results were observed by ultraviolet light box. Quantitative analysis was performed by a fluorescence immunochromatography analyzer. The results of T and C lines were expressed by converting fluorescence intensity into integral area. The standard curve for the detection of AFB1 was expressed by the relationship between the fluorescence signal intensity ratio of T line to C line ST/SC and AFB1 concentration. As to S. aureus, the standard curve was obtained by fitting the fluorescence signal intensity ratio of T line and the concentration of S. aureus. To examine the specificity, interfering species were added into the test strips in place of AFB1 or S. aureus with the same experimental procedures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and detection principles of the CD-MSN-based FLFAs
ce:section.ce:para: The route for constructing the CD-MSN fluorescent probes and the corresponding LFA design principles for AFB1 and S. aureus are shown in Scheme 1 , with CD-MSNs fabrication and biomodification illustrated in Scheme 1a and 1b. Competitive and sandwich models are routinely adopted when constructing LFAs for AFB1 and S. aureus sensing, respectively. In the former (Scheme 1c), artificial antigens AFB1-BSA and goat anti-mouse IgG were fixed on the T and C lines, respectively. The probe combines with AFB1-BSA on the T line in the absence of AFB1 (negative), while it competes with the probe in the presence of AFB1 (positive), which reduces the fluorescence intensity of the T line; this reduced intensity is the foundation of AFB1 quantification. Meanwhile, excess fluid moves forward and the probe specifically binds goat anti-mouse IgG on the C line. In the sandwich model (Scheme 1d), the CD-MSN-Van probe binds to S. aureus through strong hydrogen bonding between the d-Ala-d-Ala-termini of Van and S. aureus (Walsh, 2000), and is captured by the anti-S. aureus antibody on the T-line (positive sample). The fluorescence intensity of the T line enhances with increasing S. aureus numbers, which is the basis for quantitative S. aureus screening.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of silanized CDs, DMSNs, and CD-MSNs
ce:section.ce:para: Silanized CDs were first prepared by a facile hydrothermal method (Tong et al., 2020) using Rose Bengal (RB) and a silane coupling agent (e.g., TMS-EDA) as the raw materials (see details in the Supplementary Information (SI)). As shown in Fig. 1 a and 1b, the harvested CDs are very monodispersed, have uniform spherical morphologies, and are very small (3.3 ± 0.2 nm). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the silanized CDs (Fig. 1c) shows an obvious band at 400–600 nm, which is mainly attributable to the π → π* and n → π* transitions of the aromatic π system associated with the fluorescein structure (Chen et al., 2018). The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 1d) show that the CDs are green-emissive with excitation-independent characteristics. The optimum excitation and emission wavelengths were determined to be 505 and 525 nm, respectively. In particular, the absolute fluorescence quantum yield was measured to be 85.1 % (Fig. S1), which was very competitive among previously reported CDs. Zeta potentials measurements revealed that the CDs are negatively charged (Fig. S2, ζ = − 16.5 mV), which is mainly attributable to abundant polar Si-O groups.

DMSNs were then harvested using a typical surfactant-templating method. The silanized CDs were covalently linked to DMSN holes by condensing the silanol groups in the presence of ammonia, with CD-MSNs nanospheres readily obtained in this manner. The advantage of this combination strategy is the lack of CD leakage from the pores of the CD-MSNs. TEM, energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS), and FTIR spectroscopy were used to characterise the morphology, size, composition, and surface state of each sample. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the DMSNs have a uniform morphology, a mesoporous nature, and present a narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 247 nm (Fig. S3); their morphologies and sizes were effectively unaltered after being embedded with CDs (Fig. 2b), which were faintly observable in the enlarged image of a selected area (Fig. 2c). Furthermore, EDS elemental analyses and maps (Fig. 2d, 2e, and S4) reveal that the C content increased significantly (from 13.83 % to 31.88 %) during this process, consistent with linkages formed between the CDs and DMSNs. FTIR spectroscopy was used to confirm that the CDs were embedded. Compared to the FTIR spectrum of the DMSNs, that of the CD-MSNs shows an emerging CH asymmetric stretching absorption (2850–2960 cm−1) and a declining Si-OH vibrational stretching absorption at 962 cm−1 (Fig. 2f), consistent with Si-OH condensation and the resulting anchoring of the CDs to the DMSNs. As expected, the CD-MSN surface is positively charged (ζ = + 8.6 mV, Fig. 2g). Taken together, the above results confirm that the silanized CDs had been successful embedded in the DMSNs.

We next examined the photophysical properties of the CD-MSNs, with the concentration of embedded CDs first optimised. As shown in Fig. S5a, the fluorescence intensity of the CD-MSNs gradually increased as the CD concentration was increased from 1 to 5 mg/mL at a fixed MSN concentration (50 mg/mL) and plateaued with the further addition of CDs. Therefore, CD-MSNs synthesised at an optimised weight ratio were further characterised and used. After being CD-modified, the CD-MSNs showed absorption and fluorescence features (Fig. 2h and 2i) similar with those of the silanized CDs. In addition, the CD-MSNs have a lower absolute fluorescence quantum yield of 65.4 % (Fig. S5b) compared with that of the CDs (85.1 %, Fig. S1), which is the only observable difference. This lower quantum yield is mainly attributable to aggregate-caused quenching (ACQ) of the CDs embedded in the confined nanopores of the DMSNs (Xu, Jiang, Gao, & Kong, 2022). Even so, single CD-MSNs readily fluoresce more brightly than single CDs nanoparticles, which is favourable for bio-labelling and enhancing signal-to-noise (S/N) ratios (Huang et al., 2018; Xu et al., 2022). In addition, CD-MSNs showed similar emission properties (pH-dependent, Fig. S5c) to those of RB-based CDs (Chen et al., 2018; Tong et al., 2020). As expected, the CD-MSN nanobeads exhibited good tolerance to photobleaching (Fig. S5d). A comprehensive comparison on the photophysical and physicochemical properties between the CD-MSNs nanotags and the other luminescent labels was summarized. As shown in Table S1, the CD-MSNs exhibited overall advantages of the facile synthesis and surface modification, good photostability (no photobleaching and leakage) and physicochemical inertness. Such features will make the CD-MSNs to be promising fluorescence labels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Demonstrating CD-MSN surface modification and bioconjugation
ce:section.ce:para: Surface functional-group modification is essential for ensuring colloidal stability and surface ligand linkages. Herein, functional groups and biomolecules were covalently modified onto the surfaces of the CD-MSNs, with detailed procedures provided in the SI. As shown in Fig. 3 a, two absorption peaks centred at 1705 and 1638 cm−1 that correspond to CO stretching vibrations are obviously observed, as well as CH asymmetric stretching (2850–2930 cm−1) peaks, which greatly verified the successful amide condensation and subsequent carboxyl grafting. In addition, the surfaces of the COOH-CD-MSNs are negatively charged (ζ = −15.0 mV, Fig. 3b). These results demonstrate that the CD-MSNs had been functionalized by carboxyl. Moreover, Fig. 3c reveals that such facile modification led to no obvious variations in CD-MSNs fluorescence emissions. Finally, the label probes were prepared by covalently conjugating the CD-MSNs with AFB1 monoclonal antibody (mAb) and Van, respectively. In comparison with the zeta potential of COOH-CD-MSNs, CD-MSNs-mAb (ζ = −3.9 mV) and CD-MSNs-Van (ζ = −31.7 mV) show depressed and increased negative charge (Fig. 3b), respectively, implying successful bioconjugation and the construction of the label probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of CD-MSN LFA test strips
ce:section.ce:para: The process for fabricating the CD-MSN test strips is presented in the SI. As a competitive LFA for AFB1, we first prepared AFB1-BSA, an artificial antigen, as verified by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (Fig. S6). AFB1-BSA conjugates and goat anti-mouse IgG antibody were fixed on the NC membrane as the test (T) and control (C) lines, respectively, and some experimental conditions were optimised before being used to quantitatively detect AFB1. Kinetics curves were constructed by recording the fluorescence intensities of the T line at various intervals (Fig. 4 a), which showed that the fluorescence signal tended to stabilise after 25 min. As shown in Fig. S7, the optimal CD-MSN conjugation weight (versus 20 µg of mAb) and artificial AFB1-BSA antigen concentration at the T line are 2 mg and 0.2 mg/mL, respectively. The test strips revealed a negatively correlated linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio of the T line to the C line (ST/SC) and the logarithm of the AFB1 concentration in the 0.05–10 ng/mL range under the above optimal conditions (Fig. 4b). In addition, other interfering species, including AFB2, ZEN, OTA, AAP, KAN were included to determine the selectivity of the test strip toward AFB1. Fig. 4c reveals no significant decrement in ST/SC (excluding AFB1), which highlights the good specificity of this immune-recognition-based test strip. The analytical performance of recently published methods for the detection of AFB1 and our test strip is compared in Table S2, which shows that the developed LFA test strip based on CD-MSN label probes exhibits competitive sensitivity and excellent selectivity for AFB1, which is mainly attributable to the superior fluorescence characteristics of the CD-MSNs (e.g., high emissivity and a lack of photobleaching) and the immune-recognition design, respectively.

Furthermore, to demonstrate the potential of this CD-MSN probe for biomacromolecule or pathogen detection, a sandwich model was designed and subjected to detect S. aureus. Van is a glycopeptide antibiotic that specifically interacts with gram-positive bacteria, such as S. aureus, through hydrogen bonding at its d-Ala-d-Ala-termini (Walsh, 2000). Based on this specific mode of molecular recognition, a label probe was prepared by covalently conjugated the CD-MSNs with Van to give CD-MSNs-Van (see Fig. 3b). According to the sandwich model, the antibody was coated on the test line (T line) and the label probe and S. aureus in the sample formed antibody-target-probe sandwich complexes that enhanced the fluorescence signal of the T line. As expected, fluorescence was observed to increase linearly with the logarithm of the S. aureus concentration in the 102 to 106 cfu/mL range under the optimum conditions (Fig. 4d, 4e, and S8). We also assessed the selectivity of the test strip; Fig. 4f shows that, compared with other selected interfering pathogens, only S. aureus resulted in remarkable fluorescence enhancement, which clearly verifies that the test strip is highly specific. Table S3 summarises the analytical performance of recently developed test strips for S. aureus, which shows that the developed CD-MSN test strip exhibits very competitive sensitivity, even when compared with unique label probes, such as lanthanide-doped UCPs and time-resolution microspheres, as examples (Ali et al., 2022; Huang et al., 2020; Shi, Yang, He, & Fu, 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rapid AFB1 and S. aureus screening in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: AFB1 and S. aureus were assayed in real samples to determine how practical the fabricated test strips are. Peanuts and soybeans were chosen as test food samples to quantitatively screen for AFB1 (food sample pre-treatment is described in the SI). Table 1 shows that no AFB1 was detected in the chosen foods. Standard additions were then used to validate determinations using the standard calibration curves provided above. Acceptable recoveries of between 92.0 % and 129.0 % and acceptable standard deviations were determined for these CD-MSN test strips.

Different brands of milk purchased from local supermarkets and potable water were selected as real samples for the detection of S. aureus, the results of which are listed in Table S4. No S. aureus was detected in any sample, and standard addition experiments clearly revealed good recoveries and acceptable standard deviations. The above results clearly demonstrate that these CD-MSN-based lateral flow test strips can be used to rapidly and sensitively screen for AFB1 and S. aureus in food samples.

We developed a strategy for preparing mesoporous silicon nanoparticles covalently embedded with fluorescent carbon dots (CD-MSNs). The CD-MSNs exhibited strong and stable fluorescence emissions. Meanwhile, facile and versatile surface modification led to ideal CD-MSNs-based label probes for biomolecules. Based on competitive and sandwich model design principles, the CD-MSNs were introduced as fluorescent LFA reporters and designed to ultrasensitively detect AFB1 and S. aureus, respectively. Owing to their superior optical properties, the fabricated test strips presented comparative or lower quantitative detection limits than existing LFA methods. Furthermore, the CD-MSN test strips were shown to be practically applicable to the rapid screening of AFB1 and S. aureus in food samples, with satisfactory results obtained. More importantly, CD-MSNs can be readily developed to build LFA screening platforms for other targets by linking diverse functional ligands, such as antibodies, peptides, and nucleic acids. This work provided a new fluorescent nanobeads for biolabeling and immunoassay. Through inlaying CDs with different emissive wavelengths into the DMSNs, these multi-colour CD-MSNs nanobeads would open more opportunities to develop multiplex detection/imaging, fluorescent coding, flow cytometry, and so on.

Yuhui Wang: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Chen Deng: Methodology. Sihua Qian: Resources, Validation. Hui Li: Resources, Validation. Pan Fu: Resources, Validation. Hualan Zhou: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jianping Zheng: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LY20B050003), Ningbo 3315 Innovation Teams Program (2019A-14-C), and Ningbo Science Technology Innovation 2025 Major Research Project (2018B10054).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.133970.
